planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate in spring. Late Goldenrod spreads aggressively by rhizomes in moist, fertile conditions — best used in naturalized settings or managed by dividing every 2–3 years.

directions: Late Goldenrod is one of the most common and widespread native goldenrods of moist, open habitats across North America, blooming later than most goldenrods — from August through October — and providing one of the most important late-season nectar sources for migrating monarch butterflies, native bumblebees, sweat bees, and the last generation of many butterfly species before winter. The smooth stems (a distinguishing feature from the rough-stemmed Solidago rugosa and tall Solidago altissima) support arching plumes of bright yellow flowers that brighten roadsides, stream banks, wet meadows, and moist open disturbed areas from coast to coast. An important host for over 100 species of caterpillars and one of the most ecologically productive native plants in North America by insect diversity supported, Late Goldenrod spreads vigorously by rhizomes in moist soil and is best used in large naturalized plantings or meadow gardens where its spreading habit is an asset. Direct sow seeds in fall or divide established clumps in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall in dry to moderately moist, rocky or sandy, acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun. Silverrod is uniquely adapted to the dry, shaded conditions of rocky woodland understories where most goldenrods will not thrive — do not plant in wet, heavy, or overly fertile soil.

directions: Silverrod is the only native goldenrod in eastern North America with white flowers — a distinctive botanical curiosity in a genus that otherwise produces exclusively yellow blooms — and also the most shade-tolerant member of the group, growing in dry to moderately moist, rocky or sandy woodland understories, cliff edges, and open barrens where other goldenrods cannot establish. The wiry stems produce small spikes of creamy-white flowers in late summer and fall that provide nectar for late-season native bees and small butterflies at a time when shade garden options for pollinators are limited. Native to rocky woodlands, sandy barrens, and open forest margins from Quebec south to Georgia, Silverrod is a valuable niche plant for the dry shade garden where its tolerance of drought, poor soil, and low light makes it surprisingly adaptable. Direct sow seeds in fall in dry, rocky or sandy, acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants self-seed modestly and form long-lived clumps without aggressive spreading.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in dry, rocky, sandy, or gravelly, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate in spring. Cylindrical Blazingstar is among the most drought-tolerant of all blazingstars and specifically adapted to calcareous or gravelly prairie and alvar soils.

directions: Cylindrical Blazingstar is a compact, drought-tolerant native perennial of dry prairies, rocky outcrops, calcareous alvars, and gravelly open woodlands of the Great Lakes region and Midwest, producing spikes of few, widely spaced, cylindrical purple flower heads on stiff, slender stems in July and August that are prized for cut flowers and dried arrangements as well as for their wildlife value. Notably more drought-tolerant than most blazingstars and specifically adapted to thin, rocky, calcareous or gravelly soils of limestone outcrops and dry prairie remnants, Cylindrical Blazingstar is an outstanding choice for rock gardens, xeriscapes, and dry native plant gardens across the northern United States. The compact size and long vase life of the cut flowers make it one of the most popular Liatris species among native plant enthusiasts and cut flower gardeners. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in rocky, sandy, or gravelly, well-drained, neutral to slightly acidic soil in full sun; established corms are extremely long-lived and slowly form clumps.
sources: Prairie Plants of the University of Wisconsin-Madison Arboretum

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moderately moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich woodland or edge soil in partial shade to full sun. Hoary Skullcap tolerates a range of light conditions from partial shade to full sun and is one of the more adaptable native skullcaps for garden use.

directions: Hoary Skullcap is a native perennial of upland woods, forest edges, and open thickets of the eastern United States from New York south to Georgia and west to Missouri, producing spikes of beautiful blue-violet tubular flowers on softly white-hairy (hoary) stems from June through August — an exceptionally long bloom period for a native woodland wildflower. The flowers are specialized for long-tongued native bumblebees that are the primary pollinators, fitting into the bottom lip of each bloom and triggering pollen release; the plant is also an important nectar source for several native skippers and small butterflies. Tolerating partial shade more readily than many native prairie wildflowers, Hoary Skullcap is a versatile native plant that performs well at woodland edges, in dappled shade gardens, and in moderately sunny perennial borders. Direct sow seeds in fall in moderately moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants slowly spread by short rhizomes to form modest colonies.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun. Large Skullcap adapts to a range of light and moisture conditions and is one of the more forgiving native skullcaps for garden cultivation along the southeastern coastal plain.

directions: Large Skullcap is a native perennial of the southeastern coastal plain, found in dry to mesic, open woodlands, forest margins, and roadsides from Massachusetts south to Florida and west to Texas, producing large, showy blue-violet flowers in May and June that are notably larger than most other native skullcap species — earning the common name. The paired, tubular flowers are specialized for bumblebee pollination and are attractive both in the garden and as a wildflower accent in naturalistic plantings. Adaptable to a range of soil moisture and light conditions along the southeastern coastal plain — from open sandy roadsides to woodland edges with partial shade — Large Skullcap is one of the most garden-worthy of the native Scutellaria species in the Southeast. Direct sow seeds in fall in moist, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants are moderately drought-tolerant and long-lived.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich woodland soil in partial to full shade under deciduous trees. Showy Skullcap performs best in the cool, dappled shade of eastern deciduous forest understories — it is one of the few native wildflowers that produces exceptional bloom in moderate to deep shade.

directions: Showy Skullcap is an outstanding native woodland wildflower of the central Appalachians and adjacent regions, producing some of the most vivid blue-violet flowers of any shade-garden native from May through early June — a relative rarity among plants that genuinely thrive in moderate to deep deciduous forest shade. The clear blue, two-lipped tubular flowers are produced in terminal and axillary clusters on 12–24 inch stems and are a significant nectar source for bumblebees and other long-tongued native bees that visit the forest understory in spring. Native to moist, fertile, slightly acidic, humus-rich soils of deciduous woodlands in the central Appalachian region, Showy Skullcap is an ideal native perennial for the shaded garden border, woodland path edge, or native plant shade garden where deep blue flowers in late spring fill a critical color gap. Direct sow seeds in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich woodland soil in partial to full shade; established plants are long-lived and slowly colonize appropriate shaded spots.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds or plant tubers in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade. Florida Betony spreads very aggressively by underground tubers and can become difficult to control in lawn and garden settings — intentional cultivation in a contained bed or pot is strongly recommended.

directions: Florida Betony is a native perennial herb of moist, disturbed habitats, roadsides, and open areas across Florida and the southeastern coastal plain, best known for its white, segmented, rattlesnake-rattle-shaped underground tubers — earning it the folk name 'rattlesnake weed' — that are crisp, sweet, and edible raw or cooked with a flavor reminiscent of water chestnuts. The small pink flowers produced in spring attract native bees and are mildly ornamental, though the plant is primarily known as a prolific lawn weed throughout the Southeast due to its ability to spread rapidly from fragments of its segmented tubers. As a cultivated edible herb, the tubers are harvested in late fall through early spring when the plant is dormant or growing slowly, cleaned, and eaten raw in salads, pickled, or stir-fried. Plant in contained beds to prevent uncontrolled spreading — once established in a garden, Florida Betony is virtually impossible to eradicate without repeated effort.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring or fall in dry, sandy, gravelly, or clay, well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun. Scarify seed coats with sandpaper or soak in warm water for 24 hours before planting to speed germination. Scarlet Mallow is extremely drought-tolerant once established and will not thrive in wet or overly amended soils.

directions: Scarlet Mallow is a native perennial of the western Great Plains, dry prairies, and desert grasslands from Canada south to New Mexico and Texas, producing clusters of vivid orange-red to coral flowers with a satiny sheen on low, spreading mounds of silvery-gray, lobed foliage from spring through summer. One of the most drought-tolerant of all native wildflowers, Scarlet Mallow thrives in the hottest, driest, most exposed sites — dry roadsides, eroded slopes, rock gardens, and xeric borders — where its cheerful flowers provide an extended season of orange color from May through August in full sun. The gray, star-shaped hairs that cover the entire plant give it a silvery appearance that reflects heat and reduces water loss, making it exceptionally adapted to harsh, sunny, dry conditions. Scarify the hard seed coat before planting by rubbing with sandpaper or soaking overnight; direct sow in spring or fall in dry, well-drained soil in full sun — established plants require no irrigation and resent wet or heavy soils.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall in pure, infertile, sandy, extremely well-drained, acidic soil in full sun. Jointweed is a Florida scrub and coastal plain endemic that requires the xeric, open conditions of its native sandy habitats and will not thrive in clay, amended, or irrigated garden soils.

directions: Jointweed is a delicate-looking native perennial of the sandy coastal plain scrub, sandhills, and open pine flatwoods of the southeastern United States, producing masses of tiny white to pink flowers on jointed, wiry stems from summer through fall that create a misty, cloud-like appearance and provide essential late-season nectar for monarch butterflies and many native bee species on the sandy scrub habitats where few other wildflowers bloom at the same time. A member of the buckwheat family, Jointweed is adapted to the infertile, white-sand soils of ancient Florida scrub ridges and coastal plain sandhills where it serves as a foundational component of the herbaceous layer. Like other Florida scrub plants, it benefits from periodic, low-intensity fire that removes competing vegetation and maintains the open, sunny conditions required. Direct sow seeds in fall in pure, infertile, sandy, acidic, extremely well-drained soil in full sun; established plants are long-lived and drought-tolerant once their root systems develop.
sources: Florida Wildflowers in Their Natural Communities (Walter Kingsley Taylor)

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring after temperatures warm consistently in sandy, well-drained, infertile, acidic to neutral soil in full sun. Pineland Heliotrope thrives in the dry pinelands and open sandy habitats of Florida and the Caribbean and self-seeds prolifically in hospitable conditions.

directions: Pineland Heliotrope is a native low-growing wildflower of the dry pinelands, pine flatwoods, and open sandy habitats of Florida and the Caribbean, producing a profusion of tiny yellow to creamy-white flowers on small, spreading, leafy mats throughout the warm season. A member of the borage family, the small flowers display the characteristic scorpioid cyme (coiled flower arrangement) of the heliotrope group and provide nectar for small native bees, Ceraunus Blue butterflies, and other small pollinators of the Florida pineland community. The narrow, resin-dotted, gland-bearing leaves give the plant a pleasant, somewhat spicy fragrance when brushed. Direct sow seeds in spring in sandy, well-drained, infertile, acidic soil in full sun; Pineland Heliotrope self-seeds freely in appropriate sandy conditions and is a useful groundcover for open, xeric Florida gardens where its continuous bloom and compact form fill bare sandy spaces with pollinators.
